revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/88130458|教部省]]"
revision.text: {{統合文字|祇}}'''教部省'''（きょうぶしょう）は、明治初期の[[Daijō-kan|太政官]]制度のもと、[[Religion|宗教]]統制による国民教化の目的で設置された中央官庁組織である。 == 概説 == [[Meiji (era)|明治]]5年3月14日（[[1872|1872年]][[April 21|4月21日]]）に[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]を改組し、[[Ministry of Popular Affairs|民部省]]社寺掛を併合する形で設置 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5　宗教と国家』444ページ。神祇官内に設置された{{Interlanguage link|Missionary Member|ja|宣教使}}の[[Shinto|神道]]と[[Confucianism|儒教]]を基本とした国民教導が失敗したことを受け、当時最大の宗教勢力であった[[Buddhism|仏教]]、特に[[Jōdo Shinshū|浄土真宗]]の要請によって神・儒・仏の合同布教体制が敷かれた。[[Christianity|キリスト教]]の（半ば黙認という形での）禁制解除、社寺に於ける[[Nyonin Kinsei|女人結界]]の解除など近代宗教政策を実施する一方で、[[Department of Divinities|神祇官]]が為し得なかった国民教化を実現する為に制度を設け、[[Daikyoin|三条教則]]による国民教化・[[Proclamation of the Great Religion|大教宣布]] 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5　宗教と国家』431ページ運動を行った。 教導職は[[Voluntary sector|半官半民]]の任命制であり、[[Shinkan (official)|神官]]・[[Kannushi|神職]]、[[Bhikkhu|僧侶]]などの宗教家を始め、や{{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|ja|歌人}}、[[Haiku|俳人]]なども教導職に任命された。教部省に置かれた教導職の最上位を'''教正'''とし、国民教化をより具体的に行う為、教導職の全国統括機関である[[Daikyoin|大教院]]、各府県単位の統括を行なう中教院が設置され、全国に小教院が置かれた。しかしながら、当時新進であった神道勢力と旧来よりの勢力を保っていた浄土真宗との深刻な意見対立によって実績は揚がらぬまま、浄土真宗の大教院離脱を経て、明治10年（[[1877|1877年]]）[[January 11|1月11日]]に廃止、機能は[[Home Ministry|内務省]]{{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}へ移された。 == 教部省を巡る問題 == 神祇官・宣教使の宣教政策の失敗を受けて出来上がった教部省であったが、最終的には廃止という結果となった。その原因として当時の宗教行政の混乱が挙げられる。政府は当初、伝統宗教の権威と地盤を以ってキリスト教防御と維新後の新体制を国民に教導する考えであったが、[[Late modern period|近代]][[State (polity)|国家]]の樹立の為には[[Separation of church and state|政教分離]]と[[Freedom of religion|信教の自由]]を国家の基本方針として盛り込むことは避けられず、また西洋諸国との外交関係に於いてキリスト教の禁制解除は不可欠となった。また、{{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}の整備によって宣教政策の目的が達成されるとの観点から、政府は教部省の存在自体を懐疑的に捉える結末となり、上記の混乱によって既存の宗教勢力は右往左往することとなった。そのような混乱の中、[[Shinbutsu bunri|神仏分離]]で劣勢に立たされていた仏教勢力、特に[[Meiji Restoration|明治維新]]に際して{{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}}を支援した浄土真宗は巧みな政治工作によって教部省を政府に樹立させたが、[[Ikkō-shū|宗名問題]]（浄土真宗が公式に否認している「一向宗」を宗派名として強要しようとした問題）を機に浄土真宗内部からも政府への反発が高まってきたこともあり、教部省の施策が暗礁に乗り上げると政教分離の観点から脱退するといった動きを見せた。 == 大教院神殿問題 == 教導職の全国統括機関であった[[Daikyoin|大教院]]は東京芝にある[[Zōjō-ji|増上寺]]内に設置された。大教院の設置は仏教主導で行われた事もあり、増上寺の施設献納も自発的なものだったが、神道勢力が主導権を握りはじめると、献納を拒否する動きをみせた。しかし、増上寺の献納は予定通り進められ、なかば強引に増上寺本堂は大教院として召し上げられ、大教院神殿の拝殿として用いられた。更には神殿で行なわれる祭祀に教導職である僧侶の参列と拝礼を義務付けた為に仏教勢力の一部を激しく怒らせる結果となった（又、その反面に幟を掲げるなどして熱烈な祝福を示した僧侶もいた）。その後、仏堂の中に神社が設けられた事に反発した廃仏主義者の旧薩摩藩士によって[[Meiji (era)|明治]]7年（[[1874|1874年]]）1月1日に放火され、旧増上寺本堂は全焼したが神体はかろうじて助け出された。この神体は[[Shiba Tōshō-gū|芝東照宮]]に一時奉遷され、神道勢力が新たに設置した{{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}の神殿に遷された。 == 官員 == ; 教部卿 * 嵯峨実愛（1872年） * [[Ōki Takatō|大木喬任]]（1872年 - 1873年） ; 教部大輔 * 福羽美静（前任神祇大輔　-1872年） * 宍戸璣（1872年 - 1877年） ; 教導職 * 教導職を参照。 == 註 == == 関連項目 == * {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto|国家神道]] * [[Department of Divinities|神祇官]] * [[State religion|国家宗教]] [[Category:1877 disestablishments]] [[Category:Government agencies established in 1872]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

revision.text: ''''Kyōbu-shō'''' is a central government organization established under the [[Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[Religion|religion]] control. == 概説 == [[Meiji (era)|明治]]5年3月14日（[[1872|1872年]][[April 21|4月21日]]）に[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]を改組し、[[Ministry of Popular Affairs|民部省]]社寺掛を併合する形で設置 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5　宗教と国家』444ページ。神祇官内に設置された{{Interlanguage link|Missionary Member|ja|宣教使}}の[[Shinto|神道]]と[[Confucianism|儒教]]を基本とした国民教導が失敗したことを受け、当時最大の宗教勢力であった[[Buddhism|仏教]]、特に[[Jōdo Shinshū|浄土真宗]]の要請によって神・儒・仏の合同布教体制が敷かれた。[[Christianity|キリスト教]]の（半ば黙認という形での）禁制解除、社寺に於ける[[Nyonin Kinsei|女人結界]]の解除など近代宗教政策を実施する一方で、[[Department of Divinities|神祇官]]が為し得なかった国民教化を実現する為に制度を設け、[[Daikyoin|三条教則]]による国民教化・[[Proclamation of the Great Religion|大教宣布]] 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5　宗教と国家』431ページ運動を行った。 教導職は[[Voluntary sector|半官半民]]の任命制であり、[[Shinkan (official)|神官]]・[[Kannushi|神職]]、[[Bhikkhu|僧侶]]などの宗教家を始め、や{{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|ja|歌人}}、[[Haiku|俳人]]なども教導職に任命された。教部省に置かれた教導職の最上位を'''教正'''とし、国民教化をより具体的に行う為、教導職の全国統括機関である[[Daikyoin|大教院]]、各府県単位の統括を行なう中教院が設置され、全国に小教院が置かれた。しかしながら、当時新進であった神道勢力と旧来よりの勢力を保っていた浄土真宗との深刻な意見対立によって実績は揚がらぬまま、浄土真宗の大教院離脱を経て、明治10年（[[1877|1877年]]）[[January church and state|政教分離]]と[[Freedom of religion|信教の自由]]を国家の基本方針として盛り込むことは避けられず、また西洋諸国との外交関係に於いてキリスト教の禁制解除は不可欠となった。また、{{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}の整備によって宣教政策の目的が達成されるとの観点から、政府は教部省の存在自体を懐疑的に捉える結末となり、上記の混乱によって既存の宗教勢力は右往左往することとなった。そのような混乱の中、[[Shinbutsu bunri|神仏分離]]で劣勢に立たされていた仏教勢力、特に[[Meiji Restoration|明治維新]]に際して{{Interlanguage revision.text:
''''Kyōbu-shō'''' is a central government organization established under the [[Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[Religion|religion]] control.
== 概説 == [[Meiji (era)|明治]]5年[[14th day of the 3rd month in the Chinese calendar|3月14日]]（[[1872|1872年]][[April 21|4月21日]]）に[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]を改組し、[[Ministry of Popular Affairs|民部省]]社寺掛を併合する形で設置<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』444ページ</ref>。神祇官内に設置された{{Interlanguage link|Missionary Member|ja|宣教使}}の[[Shinto|神道]]と[[Confucianism|儒教]]を基本とした国民教導が失敗したことを受け、当時最大の宗教勢力であった[[Buddhism|仏教]]、特に[[Jōdo Shinshū|浄土真宗]]の要請によって神・儒・仏の合同布教体制が敷かれた。[[Christianity|キリスト教]]の（半ば黙認という形での）禁制解除、社寺に於ける[[Nyonin Kinsei|女人結界]]の解除など近代宗教政策を実施する一方で、[[Department of Divinities|神祇官]]が為し得なかった国民教化を実現する為に{{Interlanguage link|Kyodo Shoku|ja|教導職}}制度を設け、[[Daikyoin|三条教則]]による国民教化・[[Proclamation of the Great Religion|大教宣布]]<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref>運動を行った。
教導職は[[Voluntary sector|半官半民]]の任命制であり、[[Shinkan (official)|神官]]・[[Kannushi|神職]]、[[Bhikkhu|僧侶]]などの宗教家を始め、{{Interlanguage link|Rakugo storyteller|ja|落語家}}や{{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|ja|歌人}}、[[Haiku|俳人]]なども教導職に任命された。教部省に置かれた教導職の最上位を'''教正'''とし、国民教化をより具体的に行う為、教導職の全国統括機関である[[Daikyoin|大教院]]、各府県単位の統括を行なう中教院が設置され、全国に小教院が置かれた。しかしながら、当時新進であった神道勢力と旧来よりの勢力を保っていた浄土真宗との深刻な意見対立によって実績は揚がらぬまま、浄土真宗の大教院離脱を経て、明治10年（[[1877|1877年]]）[[January 11|1月11日]]に廃止、機能は[[Home Ministry|内務省]]{{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}へ移された。
== 教部省を巡る問題 == 神祇官・宣教使の宣教政策の失敗を受けて出来上がった教部省であったが、最終的には廃止という結果となった。その原因として当時の宗教行政の混乱が挙げられる。政府は当初、伝統宗教の権威と地盤を以ってキリスト教防御と維新後の新体制を国民に教導する考えであったが、[[Late modern period|近代]][[State (polity)|国家]]の樹立の為には[[Separation of church and state|政教分離]]と[[Freedom of religion|信教の自由]]を国家の基本方針として盛り込むことは避けられず、また西洋諸国との外交関係に於いてキリスト教の禁制解除は不可欠となった。また、{{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}の整備によって宣教政策の目的が達成されるとの観点から、政府は教部省の存在自体を懐疑的に捉える結末となり、上記の混乱によって既存の宗教勢力は右往左往することとなった。そのような混乱の中、[[Shinbutsu bunri|神仏分離]]で劣勢に立たされていた仏教勢力、特に[[Meiji Restoration|明治維新]]に際して{{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}}を支援した浄土真宗は巧みな政治工作によって教部省を政府に樹立させたが、[[Ikkō-shū|宗名問題]]（浄土真宗が公式に否認している「一向宗」を宗派名として強要しようとした問題）を機に浄土真宗内部からも政府への反発が高まってきたこともあり、教部省の施策が暗礁に乗り上げると政教分離の観点から脱退するといった動きを見せた。
== 大教院神殿問題 == 教導職の全国統括機関であった[[Daikyoin|大教院]]は東京芝にある[[Zōjō-ji|増上寺]]内に設置された。大教院の設置は仏教主導で行われた事もあり、増上寺の施設献納も自発的なものだったが、神道勢力が主導権を握りはじめると、献納を拒否する動きをみせた。しかし、増上寺の献納は予定通り進められ、なかば強引に増上寺本堂は大教院として召し上げられ、大教院神殿の拝殿として用いられた。更には神殿で行なわれる祭祀に教導職である僧侶の参列と拝礼を義務付けた為に仏教勢力の一部を激しく怒らせる結果となった（又、その反面に幟を掲げるなどして熱烈な祝福を示した僧侶もいた）。その後、仏堂の中に神社が設けられた事に反発した廃仏主義者の旧薩摩藩士によって[[Meiji (era)|明治]]7年（[[1874|1874年]]）1月1日に放火され、旧増上寺本堂は全焼したが神体はかろうじて助け出された。この神体は[[Shiba Tōshō-gū|芝東照宮]]に一時奉遷され、神道勢力が新たに設置した{{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}の神殿に遷された。
== 官員 ==
; 教部卿
* 嵯峨実愛（1872年） * [[Ōki Takatō|大木喬任]]（1872年 - 1873年）
; 教部大輔
* 福羽美静（前任神祇大輔 -1872年） * 宍戸璣（1872年 - 1877年）
; 教導職
* 教導職を参照。
== 註 == <references />
== 関連項目 ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto|国家神道]] * [[Department of Divinities|神祇官]] * [[State religion|国家宗教]] [[Category:1877 disestablishments]] [[Category:Government agencies established in 1872]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

''''Kyōbu-shō'''' is a central government organization established under the [[Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[Religion|religion]] control.
== 概説 == [[Meiji (era)|明治]]5年[[14th day of the 3rd month in the Chinese calendar|3月14日]]（[[1872|1872年]][[April 21|4月21日]]）に[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]を改組し、[[Ministry of Popular Affairs|民部省]]社寺掛を併合する形で設置<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』444ページ</ref>。神祇官内に設置された{{Interlanguage link|Missionary Member|ja|宣教使}}の[[Shinto|神道]]と[[Confucianism|儒教]]を基本とした国民教導が失敗したことを受け、当時最大の宗教勢力であった[[Buddhism|仏教]]、特に[[Jōdo Shinshū|浄土真宗]]の要請によって神・儒・仏の合同布教体制が敷かれた。[[Christianity|キリスト教]]の（半ば黙認という形での）禁制解除、社寺に於ける[[Nyonin Kinsei|女人結界]]の解除など近代宗教政策を実施する一方で、[[Department of Divinities|神祇官]]が為し得なかった国民教化を実現する為に{{Interlanguage link|Kyodo Shoku|ja|教導職}}制度を設け、[[Daikyoin|三条教則]]による国民教化・[[Proclamation of the Great Religion|大教宣布]]<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref>運動を行った。
教導職は[[Voluntary sector|半官半民]]の任命制であり、[[Shinkan (official)|神官]]・[[Kannushi|神職]]、[[Bhikkhu|僧侶]]などの宗教家を始め、{{Interlanguage link|Rakugo storyteller|ja|落語家}}や{{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|ja|歌人}}、[[Haiku|俳人]]なども教導職に任命された。教部省に置かれた教導職の最上位を'''教正'''とし、国民教化をより具体的に行う為、教導職の全国統括機関である[[Daikyoin|大教院]]、各府県単位の統括を行なう中教院が設置され、全国に小教院が置かれた。しかしながら、当時新進であった神道勢力と旧来よりの勢力を保っていた浄土真宗との深刻な意見対立によって実績は揚がらぬまま、浄土真宗の大教院離脱を経て、明治10年（[[1877|1877年]]）[[January 11|1月11日]]に廃止、機能は[[Home Ministry|内務省]]{{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}へ移された。
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Teaching == The Ministry of Religious Affairs was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish the [[Late modern period|modernity]] [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state| Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion|freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religious Affairs, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of the {{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|school system}}, and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Sembutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|en However, the [[Ikkō-shū|Mission Name Issue]] (an attempt to force the name of the Ikkyō-shū sect, officially rejected by the Jōdo Shinshu, onto the name of the sect) led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
神祇官・宣教使の宣教政策の失敗を受けて出来上がった教部省であったが、最終的には廃止という結果となった。その原因として当時の宗教行政の混乱が挙げられる。政府は当初、伝統宗教の権威と地盤を以ってキリスト教防御と維新後の新体制を国民に教導する考えであったが、[[Late modern period|近代]][[State (polity)|国家]]の樹立の為には[[Separation of church and state|政教分離]]と[[Freedom of religion|信教の自由]]を国家の基本方針として盛り込むことは避けられず、また西洋諸国との外交関係に於いてキリスト教の禁制解除は不可欠となった。また、{{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}の整備によって宣教政策の目的が達成されるとの観点から、政府は教部省の存在自体を懐疑的に捉える結末となり、上記の混乱によって既存の宗教勢力は右往左往することとなった。そのような混乱の中、[[Shinbutsu bunri|神仏分離]]で劣勢に立たされていた仏教勢力、特に[[Meiji Restoration|明治維新]]に際して{{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}}を支援した浄土真宗は巧みな政治工作によって教部省を政府に樹立させたが、[[Ikkō-shū|宗名問題]]（浄土真宗が公式に否認している「一向宗」を宗派名として強要しようとした問題）を機に浄土真宗内部からも政府への反発が高まってきたこともあり、教部省の施策が暗礁に乗り上げると政教分離の観点から脱退するといった動きを見せた。
== 大教院神殿問題 == 教導職の全国統括機関であった[[Daikyoin|大教院]]は東京芝にある[[Zōjō-ji|増上寺]]内に設置された。大教院の設置は仏教主導で行われた事もあり、増上寺の施設献納も自発的なものだったが、神道勢力が主導権を握りはじめると、献納を拒否する動きをみせた。しかし、増上寺の献納は予定通り進められ、なかば強引に増上寺本堂は大教院として召し上げられ、大教院神殿の拝殿として用いられた。更には神殿で行なわれる祭祀に教導職である僧侶の参列と拝礼を義務付けた為に仏教勢力の一部を激しく怒らせる結果となった（又、その反面に幟を掲げるなどして熱烈な祝福を示した僧侶もいた）。その後、仏堂の中に神社が設けられた事に反発した廃仏主義者の旧薩摩藩士によって[[Meiji (era)|明治]]7年（[[1874|1874年]]）1月1日に放火され、旧増上寺本堂は全焼したが神体はかろうじて助け出された。この神体は[[Shiba Tōshō-gū|芝東照宮]]に一時奉遷され、神道勢力が新たに設置した{{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}の神殿に遷された。
== 官員 ==
; 教部卿
* 嵯峨実愛（1872年） * [[Ōki Takatō|大木喬任]]（1872年 - 1873年）
; 教部大輔
* 福羽美静（前任神祇大輔 -1872年） * 宍戸璣（1872年 - 1877年）
; 教導職
* 教導職を参照。
== 註 == <references />
== 関連項目 ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto|国家神道]] * [[Department of Divinities|神祇官]] * [[State religion|国家宗教]] [[Category:1877 disestablishments]] [[Category:Government agencies established in 1872]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

''''Kyōbu-shō'''' is a central government organization established under the [[Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[Religion|religion]] control.
== 概説 == [[Meiji (era)|明治]]5年[[14th day of the 3rd month in the Chinese calendar|3月14日]]（[[1872|1872年]][[April 21|4月21日]]）に[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]を改組し、[[Ministry of Popular Affairs|民部省]]社寺掛を併合する形で設置<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』444ページ</ref>。神祇官内に設置された{{Interlanguage link|Missionary Member|ja|宣教使}}の[[Shinto|神道]]と[[Confucianism|儒教]]を基本とした国民教導が失敗したことを受け、当時最大の宗教勢力であった[[Buddhism|仏教]]、特に[[Jōdo Shinshū|浄土真宗]]の要請によって神・儒・仏の合同布教体制が敷かれた。[[Christianity|キリスト教]]の（半ば黙認という形での）禁制解除、社寺に於ける[[Nyonin Kinsei|女人結界]]の解除など近代宗教政策を実施する一方で、[[Department of Divinities|神祇官]]が為し得なかった国民教化を実現する為に{{Interlanguage link|Kyodo Shoku|ja|教導職}}制度を設け、[[Daikyoin|三条教則]]による国民教化・[[Proclamation of the Great Religion|大教宣布]]<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref>運動を行った。
教導職は[[Voluntary sector|半官半民]]の任命制であり、[[Shinkan (official)|神官]]・[[Kannushi|神職]]、[[Bhikkhu|僧侶]]などの宗教家を始め、{{Interlanguage link|Rakugo storyteller|ja|落語家}}や{{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|ja|歌人}}、[[Haiku|俳人]]なども教導職に任命された。教部省に置かれた教導職の最上位を'''教正'''とし、国民教化をより具体的に行う為、教導職の全国統括機関である[[Daikyoin|大教院]]、各府県単位の統括を行なう中教院が設置され、全国に小教院が置かれた。しかしながら、当時新進であった神道勢力と旧来よりの勢力を保っていた浄土真宗との深刻な意見対立によって実績は揚がらぬまま、浄土真宗の大教院離脱を経て、明治10年（[[1877|1877年]]）[[January 11|1月11日]]に廃止、機能は[[Home Ministry|内務省]]{{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}へ移された。
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Teaching == The Ministry of Religious Affairs was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish the [[Late modern period|modernity]] [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state| Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion|freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religious Affairs, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of the {{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}, and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Sembutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū|Mission Name Issue]] (an attempt to force the name of the Ikkyō-shū sect, officially rejected by the Jōdo Shinshu, onto the name of the sect) led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Daikyoin Temple Issue == The [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]], which was the national governing body for the teaching ministry, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in Shiba, Tokyo. The establishment of the Daikyoin was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Daikyoin and used as the worship hall of the Daikyoin Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874|1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the {{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|en|Shinto Secretariat}}.
教導職の全国統括機関であった[[Daikyoin|大教院]]は東京芝にある[[Zōjō-ji|増上寺]]内に設置された。大教院の設置は仏教主導で行われた事もあり、増上寺の施設献納も自発的なものだったが、神道勢力が主導権を握りはじめると、献納を拒否する動きをみせた。しかし、増上寺の献納は予定通り進められ、なかば強引に増上寺本堂は大教院として召し上げられ、大教院神殿の拝殿として用いられた。更には神殿で行なわれる祭祀に教導職である僧侶の参列と拝礼を義務付けた為に仏教勢力の一部を激しく怒らせる結果となった（又、その反面に幟を掲げるなどして熱烈な祝福を示した僧侶もいた）。その後、仏堂の中に神社が設けられた事に反発した廃仏主義者の旧薩摩藩士によって[[Meiji (era)|明治]]7年（[[1874|1874年]]）1月1日に放火され、旧増上寺本堂は全焼したが神体はかろうじて助け出された。この神体は[[Shiba Tōshō-gū|芝東照宮]]に一時奉遷され、神道勢力が新たに設置した{{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}の神殿に遷された。
== 官員 ==
; 教部卿
* 嵯峨実愛（1872年） * [[Ōki Takatō|大木喬任]]（1872年 - 1873年）
; 教部大輔
* 福羽美静（前任神祇大輔 -1872年） * 宍戸璣（1872年 - 1877年）
; 教導職
* 教導職を参照。
== 註 == <references />
== 関連項目 ==

''''Kyōbu-shō'''' is a central government organization established under the [[Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[Religion|religion]] control.
== 概説 == [[Meiji (era)|明治]]5年[[14th day of the 3rd month in the Chinese calendar|3月14日]]（[[1872|1872年]][[April 21|4月21日]]）に[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]を改組し、[[Ministry of Popular Affairs|民部省]]社寺掛を併合する形で設置<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』444ページ</ref>。神祇官内に設置された{{Interlanguage link|Missionary Member|ja|宣教使}}の[[Shinto|神道]]と[[Confucianism|儒教]]を基本とした国民教導が失敗したことを受け、当時最大の宗教勢力であった[[Buddhism|仏教]]、特に[[Jōdo Shinshū|浄土真宗]]の要請によって神・儒・仏の合同布教体制が敷かれた。[[Christianity|キリスト教]]の（半ば黙認という形での）禁制解除、社寺に於ける[[Nyonin Kinsei|女人結界]]の解除など近代宗教政策を実施する一方で、[[Department of Divinities|神祇官]]が為し得なかった国民教化を実現する為に{{Interlanguage link|Kyodo Shoku|ja|教導職}}制度を設け、[[Daikyoin|三条教則]]による国民教化・[[Proclamation of the Great Religion|大教宣布]]<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref>運動を行った。
教導職は[[Voluntary sector|半官半民]]の任命制であり、[[Shinkan (official)|神官]]・[[Kannushi|神職]]、[[Bhikkhu|僧侶]]などの宗教家を始め、{{Interlanguage link|Rakugo storyteller|ja|落語家}}や{{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|ja|歌人}}、[[Haiku|俳人]]なども教導職に任命された。教部省に置かれた教導職の最上位を'''教正'''とし、国民教化をより具体的に行う為、教導職の全国統括機関である[[Daikyoin|大教院]]、各府県単位の統括を行なう中教院が設置され、全国に小教院が置かれた。しかしながら、当時新進であった神道勢力と旧来よりの勢力を保っていた浄土真宗との深刻な意見対立によって実績は揚がらぬまま、浄土真宗の大教院離脱を経て、明治10年（[[1877|1877年]]）[[January 11|1月11日]]に廃止、機能は[[Home Ministry|内務省]]{{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}へ移された。
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Teaching == The Ministry of Religious Affairs was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish the [[Late modern period|modernity]] [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state| Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion|freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religious Affairs, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of the {{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}, and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Sembutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū|Mission Name Issue]] (an attempt to force the name of the Ikkyō-shū sect, officially rejected by the Jōdo Shinshu, onto the name of the sect) led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Daikyoin Temple Issue == The [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]], which was the national governing body for the teaching ministry, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in Shiba, Tokyo. The establishment of the Daikyoin was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Daikyoin and used as the worship hall of the Daikyoin Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874|1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the {{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}.
== 官員 ==
; 教部卿
* 嵯峨実愛（1872年） * [[Ōki Takatō|大木喬任]]（1872年 - 1873年）
; 教部大輔
* 福羽美静（前任神祇大輔 -1872年） * 宍戸璣（1872年 - 1877年）
; 教導職
* 教導職を参照。
== References == <references />
== 関連項目 ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto|国家神道]] * [[Department of Divinities|神祇官]] * [[State religion|国家宗教]] [[Category:1877 disestablishments]] [[Category:Government agencies established in 1872]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

== References == <references />
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]] [[Category:1877 disestablishments]] [[Category:Government agencies established in 1872]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

''''Kyōbu-shō'''' is a central government organization established under the [[Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[Religion|religion]] control.
== Outline == In [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]]5 [[14th day of the 3rd month in the Chinese calendar]] ([[1872|1872]] [[April 21|April 21]]), the [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinity]] was reorganized. Ministry of Popular Affairs|Ministry of Civil Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs|Minbusho]] Shakaiji Kake<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref>. Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism|Confucianism]] by the {{Interlanguage link|Missionary Member|en|missionary}} established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[[ Buddhism|Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity|Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei|Woman's Boundary]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities|Divine Councilor]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the [[Interlocutors]]. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities|Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of {{Interlanguage link|Kyodo Shoku|en}} was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Sanjo Kyōritsu]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Religion]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The teaching ministry was a [[Voluntary sector|semi-private sector]] appointment system, and included religious figures such as [[Shinkan (official)|priest]]s, [[Kannushi|priest]]s, [[Bhikkhu|priest]]s, {{Interlanguage link|Rakugo storyteller|en|rakugo storyteller}}, {{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|en|poet}}, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s, etc. were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was '''kyosei''', and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the Chu-kyoin to oversee each prefectural unit, and the Sub-kyoin were established throughout the country. However, the Daikyoin did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming Shintoists, and the Jodo Shinshu, who had maintained their old power. Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]]{{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|en|Shrine and Temple Bureau}}.
[[Meiji (era)|明治]]5年[[14th day of the 3rd month in the Chinese calendar|3月14日]]（[[1872|1872年]][[April 21|4月21日]]）に[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]を改組し、[[Ministry of Popular Affairs|民部省]]社寺掛を併合する形で設置<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』444ページ</ref>。神祇官内に設置された{{Interlanguage link|Missionary Member|ja|宣教使}}の[[Shinto|神道]]と[[Confucianism|儒教]]を基本とした国民教導が失敗したことを受け、当時最大の宗教勢力であった[[Buddhism|仏教]]、特に[[Jōdo Shinshū|浄土真宗]]の要請によって神・儒・仏の合同布教体制が敷かれた。[[Christianity|キリスト教]]の（半ば黙認という形での）禁制解除、社寺に於ける[[Nyonin Kinsei|女人結界]]の解除など近代宗教政策を実施する一方で、[[Department of Divinities|神祇官]]が為し得なかった国民教化を実現する為に{{Interlanguage link|Kyodo Shoku|ja|教導職}}制度を設け、[[Daikyoin|三条教則]]による国民教化・[[Proclamation of the Great Religion|大教宣布]]<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref>運動を行った。
教導職は[[Voluntary sector|半官半民]]の任命制であり、[[Shinkan (official)|神官]]・[[Kannushi|神職]]、[[Bhikkhu|僧侶]]などの宗教家を始め、{{Interlanguage link|Rakugo storyteller|ja|落語家}}や{{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|ja|歌人}}、[[Haiku|俳人]]なども教導職に任命された。教部省に置かれた教導職の最上位を'''教正'''とし、国民教化をより具体的に行う為、教導職の全国統括機関である[[Daikyoin|大教院]]、各府県単位の統括を行なう中教院が設置され、全国に小教院が置かれた。しかしながら、当時新進であった神道勢力と旧来よりの勢力を保っていた浄土真宗との深刻な意見対立によって実績は揚がらぬまま、浄土真宗の大教院離脱を経て、明治10年（[[1877|1877年]]）[[January 11|1月11日]]に廃止、機能は[[Home Ministry|内務省]]{{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}へ移された。

== The Daikyoin Temple Issue == The [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]], which was the national governing body for the teaching ministry, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in Shiba, Tokyo. The establishment of the Daikyoin was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Daikyoin and used as the worship hall of the Daikyoin Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874|1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the {{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}.
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872 - 1873)
; Daisuke Kobe
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872 - 1877)
; Teaching position
* Reference to the teaching position.
== References == <references />
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]] [[Category:1877 disestablishments]] [[Category:Government agencies established in 1872]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

revision.comment: /* top */
revision.text:
''''Kyōbu-shō'''' is a central government organization established under the [[Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[Religion|religion]] control.
== Outline == In [[1872|1872]] [[April 21|April 21]], the [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinity]] was reorganized. Ministry of Popular Affairs|Ministry of Civil Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs|Minbusho]] Shakaiji Kake<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref>. Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism|Confucianism]] by the {{Interlanguage link|Missionary Member|ja|宣教使}} established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity|Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei|Woman's Boundary]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities|Divine Councilor]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the [[Interlocutors]]. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities|Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of {{Interlanguage link|Kyodo Shoku|ja|教導職}} was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Sanjo Kyōritsu]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Religion]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The teaching ministry was a [[Voluntary sector|semi-private sector]] appointment system, and included religious figures such as [[Shinkan (official)|priest]]s, [[Kannushi|priest]]s, [[Bhikkhu|priest]]s, {{Interlanguage link|Rakugo storyteller|ja|落語家}}, {{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|ja|歌人}}, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s, etc. were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was '''kyosei''', and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the Chu-kyoin to oversee each prefectural unit, and the Sub-kyoin were established throughout the country. However, the Daikyoin did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming Shintoists, and the Jodo Shinshu, who had maintained their old power. [[Home Ministry]]{{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Teaching == The Ministry of Religious Affairs was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish the [[Late modern period|modernity]] [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state| Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion|freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religious Affairs, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of the {{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}, and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Sembutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū|Mission Name Issue]] (an attempt to force the name of the Ikkyō-shū sect, officially rejected by the Jōdo Shinshu, onto the name of the sect) led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Daikyoin Temple Issue == The [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]], which was the national governing body for the teaching ministry, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in Shiba, Tokyo. The establishment of the Daikyoin was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Daikyoin and used as the worship hall of the Daikyoin Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874|1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the {{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}.
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872 - 1873)
; Daisuke Kobe
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872 - 1877)
; Teaching position
* Reference to the teaching position.
== References == <references />
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]] [[Category:1877 disestablishments]] [[Category:Government agencies established in 1872]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Teaching == The Ministry of Religious Affairs was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish the [[Late modern period|modernity]] [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state| Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion|freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religious Affairs, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of the {{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}, and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Sembutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū|Mission Name Issue]] (an attempt to force the name of the Ikkyō-shū sect, officially rejected by the Jōdo Shinshu, onto the name of the sect) led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Daikyoin Temple Issue == The [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]], which was the national governing body for the teaching ministry, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in Shiba, Tokyo. The establishment of the Daikyoin was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Daikyoin and used as the worship hall of the Daikyoin Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874|1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the {{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}.
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872 - 1873)
; Daisuke Kobe
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872 - 1877)
; Teaching position
* Reference to the teaching position.
== References == <references />
== See Also ==

revision.comment: /* Outline */
revision.text:
The {{Nihongo|Ministry of Education|教部省|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization established under the [[Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[Religion|religion]] control.
== Outline == In [[1872|1872]] [[April 21|April 21]], the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was reorganized. [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs|Minbusho]] Shakaiji Kake<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref>. Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism|Confucianism]] by the {{Interlanguage link|Missionary Member|ja|宣教使}} established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity|Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei|Woman's Boundary]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities|Divine Councilor]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities|Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of {{Interlanguage link|Kyodo Shoku|ja|教導職}} was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Sanjo Kyōritsu]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Religion]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The teaching ministry was a [[Voluntary sector|semi-private sector]] appointment system, and included religious figures such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|Rakugo storyteller|ja|落語家}}, {{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|ja|歌人}}, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s, etc. were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was '''kyosei''', and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the Chu-kyoin to oversee each prefectural unit, and the Sub-kyoin were established throughout the country. However, the Daikyoin did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming Shintoists, and the Jodo Shinshu, who had maintained their old power. [[Home Ministry]]{{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Teaching == The Ministry of Religious Affairs was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish the [[Late modern period|modernity]] [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state| Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion|freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religious Affairs, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of the {{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}, and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Sembutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū|Mission Name Issue]] (an attempt to force the name of the Ikkyō-shū sect, officially rejected by the Jōdo Shinshu, onto the name of the sect) led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Daikyoin Temple Issue == The [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]], which was the national governing body for the teaching ministry, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in Shiba, Tokyo. The establishment of the Daikyoin was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Daikyoin and used as the worship hall of the Daikyoin Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874|1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the {{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}.
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872 - 1873)
; Daisuke Kobe
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872 - 1877)
; Teaching position
* Reference to the teaching position.
== References == <references />
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]] [[Category:1877 disestablishments]] [[Category:Government agencies established in 1872]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs|Minbusho]] Shakaiji Kake<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref>. Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism|Confucianism]] by the {{Interlanguage link|Missionary Messenger (Japan)|ja|宣教使}} established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity|Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei|Woman's Boundary]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities|Divine Councilor]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities|Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of {{Interlanguage link|Kyodo Shoku|ja|教導職}} was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Sanjo Kyōritsu]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Religion]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The teaching ministry was a [[Voluntary sector]] appointment system, and included religious figures such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|Rakugo storyteller|lt=Rakugo storytellers|ja|落語家}}, {{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|lt=Waka-Tanka Poets|ja|歌人}}, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s, etc. were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was '''kyosei''', and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the Chu-kyoin to oversee each prefectural unit, and the Sub-kyoin were established throughout the country. However, the Daikyoin did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming Shintoists, and the Jodo Shinshu, who had maintained their old power. [[Home Ministry]]{{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Teaching == The Ministry of Religious Affairs was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish the [[Late modern period|modernity]] [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state| Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion|freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religious Affairs, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of the {{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}, and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Sembutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū|Mission Name Issue]] (an attempt to force the name of the Ikkyō-shū sect, officially rejected by the Jōdo Shinshu, onto the name of the sect) led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Daikyoin Temple Issue == The [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]], which was the national governing body for the teaching ministry, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in Shiba, Tokyo. The establishment of the Daikyoin was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Daikyoin and used as the worship hall of the Daikyoin Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874|1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the {{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}.
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872 - 1873)
; Daisuke Kobe
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872 - 1877)
; Teaching position
* Reference to the teaching position.
== References == <references />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]
[[Category:1877 disestablishments]] [[Category:Government agencies established in 1872]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism|Confucianism]] by the {{Interlanguage link|Missionary Messenger (Japan)|ja|宣教使}} established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], which was the largest religious force at the time. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.

Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874|1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the {{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}.
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872 - 1873)
; Daisuke Kobe
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872 - 1877)
; Teaching position
* Reference to the teaching position.
== References == <references />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]

revision.comment: Notifying subject page of move discussion on [[Talk:Ministry of Education (Imperial Japan)#Requested move 27 April 2022|Talk:Ministry of Education (Imperial Japan)]]
revision.text:
<noinclude>{{User:RMCD bot/subject notice|1=Ministry of Teachings|2=Talk:Ministry of Education (Imperial Japan)#Requested move 27 April 2022}} </noinclude>The {{Nihongo|'''Ministry of Education'''|教部省|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization established under the [[Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[Religion|religion]] control.
== Outline == In [[1872|1872]] [[April 21|April 21]], the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was reorganized. [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs|Minbusho]] Shakaiji Kake<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref>. Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism|Confucianism]] by the {{Interlanguage link|Missionary Messenger (Japan)|ja|宣教使}} established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity|Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei|Woman's Boundary]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities|Divine Councilor]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities|Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of {{Interlanguage link|Kyodo Shoku|ja|教導職}} was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Sanjo Kyōritsu]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Religion]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The teaching ministry was a [[Voluntary sector]] appointment system, and included religious figures such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, {{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|lt=Waka-Tanka Poets|ja|歌人}}, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s, etc. were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was '''kyosei''', and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the Chu-kyoin to oversee each prefectural unit, and the Sub-kyoin were established throughout the country. However, the Daikyoin did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming Shintoists, and the Jodo Shinshu, who had maintained their old power. [[Home Ministry]] {{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Teaching == The Ministry of Religious Affairs was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish the [[Late modern period|modernity]] [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state| Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion|freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religious Affairs, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of the {{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}, and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Sembutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū|Mission Name Issue]] (an attempt to force the name of the Ikkyō-shū sect, officially rejected by the Jōdo Shinshu, onto the name of the sect) led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Daikyoin Temple Issue == The [[Daikyoin|Daikyoin]], which was the national governing body for the teaching ministry, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in Shiba, Tokyo. The establishment of the Daikyoin was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Daikyoin and used as the worship hall of the Daikyoin Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874|1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the {{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}.
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872 - 1873)
; Daisuke Kobe
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872 - 1877)
; Teaching position
* Reference to the teaching position.
== References == <references />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]
[[Category:1877 disestablishments]] [[Category:Government agencies established in 1872]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The teaching ministry was a [[Voluntary sector]] appointment system, and included religious figures such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, {{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|lt=Waka-Tanka Poets|ja|歌人}}, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s, etc. were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was '''kyosei''', and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the Chu-kyoin to oversee each prefectural unit, and the Sub-kyoin were established throughout the country. However, the Daikyoin did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming Shintoists, and the Jodo Shinshu, who had maintained their old power. [[Home Ministry]] {{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Teaching == The Ministry of Religious Affairs was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish the [[Late modern period|modernity]] [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religious Affairs, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of the {{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}, and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Sembutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū|Mission Name Issue]] (an attempt to force the name of the Ikkyō-shū sect, officially rejected by the Jōdo Shinshu, onto the name of the sect) led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Daikyoin Temple Issue == The [[Daikyoin]], which was the national governing body for the teaching ministry, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in Shiba, Tokyo. The establishment of the Daikyoin was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Daikyoin and used as the worship hall of the Daikyoin Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the {{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}.
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position.
== References == <references />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]
[[Category:1877 disestablishments]] [[Category:Government agencies established in 1872]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

revision.text:
The {{Nihongo|'''Ministry of Education'''|教部省|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization established under the [[Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[religion]] control.
== Outline == In [[1872]] [[April 21]], the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was reorganized. [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs|Minbusho]] Shakaiji Kake.<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the {{Interlanguage link|Missionary Messenger (Japan)|ja|宣教使}} established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei|Woman's Boundary]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities|Divine Councilor]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities|Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of {{Interlanguage link|Kyodo Shoku|ja|教導職}} was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Sanjo Kyōritsu]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Religion]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The teaching ministry was a [[Voluntary sector]] appointment system, and included religious figures such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, {{Interlanguage link|Waka-Tanka Poet|lt=Waka-Tanka Poets|ja|歌人}}, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s, etc. were also appointed as instructors. However, the Daikyoin did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming Shintoists, and the Jodo Shinshu, who had maintained their old power. [[Home Ministry]] {{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}.

One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish the [[Late modern period|modernity]] [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religious Affairs, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of the {{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}, and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Sembutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū|Mission Name Issue]] (an attempt to force the name of the Ikkyō-shū sect, officially rejected by the Jōdo Shinshu, onto the name of the sect) led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Daikyoin Temple Issue == The [[Daikyoin]], which was the national governing body for the teaching ministry, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in Shiba, Tokyo. The establishment of the Daikyoin was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Daikyoin and used as the worship hall of the Daikyoin Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the {{Interlanguage link|Shinto Secretariat|ja|神道事務局}}.
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position.
== References == <references />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} * [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]

[[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs|Minbusho]] Shakaiji Kake.<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the [[Missionary Messenger (Japan)]] established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei|Woman's Boundary]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities|Divine Councilor]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities|Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of [[Kyodo Shoku]] was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Sanjo Kyōritsu]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Religion]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The teaching ministry was a [[Voluntary sector]] appointment system, and included religious figures such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, {{Interlanguage link|Waka poets|lt=|ja|歌人}}, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s, etc. were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was '''kyosei''', and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the Chu-kyoin to oversee each prefectural unit, and the Sub-kyoin were established throughout the country. However, the Daikyoin did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming Shintoists, and the Jodo Shinshu, who had maintained their old power. [[Home Ministry]] {{Interlanguage link|Shrines and Temples Bureau|ja|社寺局}}.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Teaching == The Ministry of Religious Affairs was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish the [[Late modern period|modernity]] [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religious Affairs, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of {{ill|Gakusei|lt=The Modern School System|ja|学制}}, and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Sembutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū|Mission Name Issue]] (an attempt to force the name of the Ikkyō-shū sect, officially rejected by the Jōdo Shinshu, onto the name of the sect) led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Daikyoin Temple Issue == The [[Daikyoin]], which was the national governing body for the teaching ministry, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in Shiba, Tokyo. The establishment of the Daikyoin was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Daikyoin and used as the worship hall of the Daikyoin Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, [[1874]], the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Shinto Secretariat]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position.
== References == <references />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Modern Japanese government system|ja|近代日本の官制}} * [[Shinto nationalization]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]
