{{Short description|Japanese government agency}} {{Distinguish|Bureau of Religions}} {{Infobox organization | formation = [[1872]] | native name = 教部省 | predecessor = [[Ministry of Divinities]], [[Missionary Office]] | successor = [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] | dissolved = [[1877]] }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ministry of Religion'''|教部省|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization of the [[Empire of Japan]] established under the [[Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[religion]] control to replace the [[Department of Divinities]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html#kyobusho |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It sought to advance the [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]] and mobilize the people with both [[Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
It was abolished in response to arguments for [[separation of church and state]] and the [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] taking over much of its functions.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
== Outline == On April 21, 1872, the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was reorganized into the Ministry of Religion. The [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]] Shakaiji Kake.{{Incomprehensible inline|date=May 2023}}<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the [[Missionary Office]] established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of [[Kyodo Shoku]] was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The Ministry of Religion was a [[voluntary sector]] appointment system. Religious figures—such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, Waka poets, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s—were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was {{Transliteration|ja|kyosei}}, and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the {{Nihongo|Chukyoin|中教院}} to oversee each prefectural unit, and the {{Nihongo|Shukyoin|小教院}} were established throughout the country. However, the Great Teaching Institute did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming, and the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], who had maintained their old power.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Religion == The Ministry of Religion was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also ==
* [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]

{{Short description|Japanese government agency}} {{Distinguish|Bureau of Religions}} {{Infobox organization | formation = [[1872]] | native name = 教部省 | predecessor = [[Ministry of Divinities]], [[Missionary Office]] | successor = [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] | dissolved = [[1877]] }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ministry of Religion'''|教部省|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization of the [[Empire of Japan]] established under the [[Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[religion]] control to replace the [[Department of Divinities]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html#kyobusho |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It sought to advance the [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]] and mobilize the people with both [[Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
It was abolished in response to arguments for [[separation of church and state]] and the [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] taking over much of its functions.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
== Outline == On April 21, 1872, the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was reorganized into the Ministry of Religion. The [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]] Shakaiji Kake.{{Incomprehensible inline|date=May 2023}}<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the [[Missionary Office]] established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of [[Kyodo Shoku]] was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The Ministry of Religion was a [[voluntary sector]] appointment system. Religious figures—such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, Waka poets, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s—were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was {{Transliteration|ja|kyosei}}, and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the {{Nihongo|Chukyoin|中教院}} to oversee each prefectural unit, and the {{Nihongo|Shukyoin|小教院}} were established throughout the country. However, the Great Teaching Institute did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming, and the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], who had maintained their old power.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Religion == The Ministry of Religion was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese government agency}} {{Distinguish|Bureau of Religions}} {{Infobox organization | formation = [[1872]] | native name = 教部省 | predecessor = [[Ministry of Divinities]], [[Missionary Office]] | successor = [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] | dissolved = [[1877]] }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ministry of Religion'''|教部省|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization of the [[Empire of Japan]] established under the [[Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[religion]] control to replace the [[Department of Divinities]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html#kyobusho |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It sought to advance the [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]] and mobilize the people with both [[Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
It was abolished in response to arguments for [[separation of church and state]] and the [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] taking over much of its functions.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
== Outline == On April 21, 1872, the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was reorganized into the Ministry of Religion. The [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]] Shakaiji Kake.{{Incomprehensible inline|date=May 2023}}<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the [[Missionary Office]] established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of [[Kyodo Shoku]] was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The Ministry of Religion was a [[voluntary sector]] appointment system. Religious figures—such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, Waka poets, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s—were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was {{Transliteration|ja|kyosei}}, and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the {{Nihongo|Chukyoin|中教院}} to oversee each prefectural unit, and the {{Nihongo|Shukyoin|小教院}} were established throughout the country. However, the Great Teaching Institute did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming, and the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], who had maintained their old power.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Religion == The Ministry of Religion was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also ==
* [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]

{{Short description|Japanese government agency}} {{Distinguish|Bureau of Religions}} {{Infobox organization | formation = [[1872]] | native name = 教部省 | predecessor = [[Ministry of Divinities]], [[Missionary Office]] | successor = [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] | dissolved = [[1877]] }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ministry of Religion'''|教部省|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization of the [[Empire of Japan]] established under the [[Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[religion]] control to replace the [[Department of Divinities]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html#kyobusho |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It sought to advance the [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]] and mobilize the people with both [[Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
It was abolished in response to arguments for [[separation of church and state]] and the [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] taking over much of its functions.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
== Outline == On April 21, 1872, the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was reorganized into the Ministry of Religion. The [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]] Shakaiji Kake.{{Incomprehensible inline|date=May 2023}}<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the [[Missionary Office]] established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of [[Kyodo Shoku]] was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The Ministry of Religion was a [[voluntary sector]] appointment system. Religious figures—such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, Waka poets, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s—were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was {{Transliteration|ja|kyosei}}, and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the {{Nihongo|Chukyoin|中教院}} to oversee each prefectural unit, and the {{Nihongo|Shukyoin|小教院}} were established throughout the country. However, the Great Teaching Institute did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming, and the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], who had maintained their old power.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Religion == The Ministry of Religion was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also ==
* [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]

The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position

{{Short description|Japanese government agency}} {{Distinguish|Bureau of Religions}} {{Infobox organization | formation = [[1872]] | native name = 教部省 | predecessor = [[Ministry of Divinities]], [[Missionary Office]] | successor = [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] | dissolved = [[1877]] }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ministry of Religion'''|教部省|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization of the [[Empire of Japan]] established under the [[Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[religion]] control to replace the [[Department of Divinities]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html#kyobusho |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It sought to advance the [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]] and mobilize the people with both [[Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
It was abolished in response to arguments for [[separation of church and state]] and the [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] taking over much of its functions.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
== Outline == On April 21, 1872, the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was reorganized into the Ministry of Religion. The [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]] Shakaiji Kake.{{Incomprehensible inline|date=May 2023}}<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the [[Missionary Office]] established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of [[Kyodo Shoku]] was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The Ministry of Religion was a [[voluntary sector]] appointment system. Religious figures—such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, Waka poets, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s—were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was {{Transliteration|ja|kyosei}}, and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the {{Nihongo|Chukyoin|中教院}} to oversee each prefectural unit, and the {{Nihongo|Shukyoin|小教院}} were established throughout the country. However, the Great Teaching Institute did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming, and the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], who had maintained their old power.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Religion == The Ministry of Religion was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position

{{Short description|Japanese government agency}} {{Distinguish|Bureau of Religions}} {{Infobox organization | formation = [[1872]] | native name = 教部省 | predecessor = [[Ministry of Divinities]], [[Missionary Office]] | successor = [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] | dissolved = [[1877]] }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ministry of Religion'''|教部省|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization of the [[Empire of Japan]] established under the [[Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[religion]] control to replace the [[Department of Divinities]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html#kyobusho |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It sought to advance the [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]] and mobilize the people with both [[Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
It was abolished in response to arguments for [[separation of church and state]] and the [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] taking over much of its functions.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
== Outline == On April 21, 1872, the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was reorganized into the Ministry of Religion. The [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]] Shakaiji Kake.{{Incomprehensible inline|date=May 2023}}<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the [[Missionary Office]] established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of [[Kyodo Shoku]] was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The Ministry of Religion was a [[voluntary sector]] appointment system. Religious figures—such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, Waka poets, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s—were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was {{Transliteration|ja|kyosei}}, and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the {{Nihongo|Chukyoin|中教院}} to oversee each prefectural unit, and the {{Nihongo|Shukyoin|小教院}} were established throughout the country. However, the Great Teaching Institute did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming, and the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], who had maintained their old power.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Religion == The Ministry of Religion was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also ==
* [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]
{{State Shinto}}

The [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]] Shakaiji Kake.{{Incomprehensible inline|date=May 2023}}<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the [[Missionary Office]] established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of [[Kyodo Shoku]] was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The Ministry of Religion was a [[voluntary sector]] appointment system. Religious figures—such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, Waka poets, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s—were also appointed as instructors. The highest rank in the Ministry of Education was {{Transliteration|ja|kyosei}}, and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the {{Nihongo|Chukyoin|中教院}} to oversee each prefectural unit, and the {{Nihongo|Shukyoin|小教院}} were established throughout the country. However, the Great Teaching Institute did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming, and the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], who had maintained their old power.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Religion == The Ministry of Religion was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also ==
* [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]

The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. The Ministry of Religion was a [[voluntary sector]] appointment system. Religious figures—such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{ill|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, Waka poets, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s—were also appointed as instructors. The highest Rank in the Ministry of Education was {{Transliteration|ja|kyosei}}, and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the {{Nihongo|Chukyoin|中教院}} to oversee each prefectural unit, and the {{Nihongo|Shukyoin|小教院}} were established throughout the country. However, the Great Teaching Institute did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming, and the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], who had maintained their old power.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Religion == The Ministry of Religion was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{ill|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also ==
* [[State Shinto]] * [[Department of Divinities]] * [[State religion]]

{{Short description|Japanese government agency}} {{Distinguish|Bureau of Religions}} {{Infobox organization | formation = [[1872]] | native name = 教部省 | predecessor = [[Ministry of Divinities]], [[Missionary Office]] | successor = [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] | dissolved = [[1877]] }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ministry of Religion'''|教部省|1=Ministry of Religion (Japan)|lt=Ministry of Religion|qq= (Japan)|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization of the [[Empire of Japan]] established under the [[Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[religion]] control to replace the [[Department of Divinities]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html#kyobusho |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It sought to advance the [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]] and mobilize the people with both [[Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
It was abolished in response to arguments for [[separation of church and state]] and the [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] taking over much of its functions.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
== Outline == On April 21, 1872, the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was reorganized into the Ministry of Religion. The [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]] Shakaiji Kake.{{Incomprehensible inline|date=May 2023}}<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the [[Missionary Office]] established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of [[Kyodo Shoku]] was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The Ministry of Religion was a [[voluntary sector]] appointment system. Religious figures—such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{ill|Rakugoka|ja|落語家}}, Waka poets, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s—were also appointed as instructors. The highest Rank in the Ministry of Education was {{Transliteration|ja|kyosei}}, and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the {{Nihongo|Chukyoin|中教院}} to oversee each prefectural unit, and the {{Nihongo|Shukyoin|小教院}} were established throughout the country. However, the Great Teaching Institute did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming, and the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], who had maintained their old power.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Religion == The Ministry of Religion was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{ill|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''

The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{ill|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Defeat the Shogunate Movement|qq=}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position

revision.comment: Bot: mark page as missing translated-page template
revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese government agency}} {{Distinguish|Bureau of Religions}} {{Infobox organization | formation = [[1872]] | native name = 教部省 | predecessor = [[Ministry of Divinities]], [[Missionary Office]] | successor = [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] | dissolved = [[1877]] }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ministry of Religion'''|教部省|1=Ministry of Religion (Japan)|lt=Ministry of Religion|qq= (Japan)|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization of the [[Empire of Japan]] established under the [[Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[religion]] control to replace the [[Department of Divinities]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html#kyobusho |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It sought to advance the [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]] and mobilize the people with both [[Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
It was abolished in response to arguments for [[separation of church and state]] and the [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] taking over much of its functions.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
== Outline == On April 21, 1872, the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was reorganized into the Ministry of Religion. The [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]] Shakaiji Kake.{{Incomprehensible inline|date=May 2023}}<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shintoism|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the [[Missionary Office]] established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of [[Kyodo Shoku]] was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The Ministry of Religion was a [[voluntary sector]] appointment system. Religious figures—such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{ill|Rakugoka|ja|落語家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakugoka|qq=}}, Waka poets, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s—were also appointed as instructors. The highest Rank in the Ministry of Education was {{Transliteration|ja|kyosei}}, and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the {{Nihongo|Chukyoin|中教院}} to oversee each prefectural unit, and the {{Nihongo|Shukyoin|小教院}} were established throughout the country. However, the Great Teaching Institute did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming, and the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], who had maintained their old power.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Religion == The Ministry of Religion was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{ill|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Defeat the Shogunate Movement|qq=}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position

When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:教部省]] [[ru:Министерство религий Японии]] [[uk:Міністерство релігій Японії]]

revision.comment: Bot: classify by valid enwiki interwikis
revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese government agency}} {{Distinguish|Bureau of Religions}} {{Infobox organization | formation = [[1872]] | native name = 教部省 | predecessor = [[Ministry of Divinities]], [[Missionary Office]] | successor = [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] | dissolved = [[1877]] }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ministry of Religion'''|教部省|1=Ministry of Religion (Japan)|lt=Ministry of Religion|qq= (Japan)|Kyōbushō}} was a central government organization of the [[Empire of Japan]] established under the [[Daijō-kan]] system in the early Meiji period for the purpose of national indoctrination through [[religion]] control to replace the [[Department of Divinities]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html#kyobusho |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It sought to advance the [[Taikyo Proclamation|Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]] and mobilize the people with both [[Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
It was abolished in response to arguments for [[separation of church and state]] and the [[Bureau of Shrines and Temples]] taking over much of its functions.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
== Outline == On April 21, 1872, the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was reorganized into the Ministry of Religion. The [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]], and established by merging the [[Ministry of Popular Affairs]] Shakaiji Kake.{{Incomprehensible inline|date=May 2023}}<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru and Masato Miyaji, Nihon Kindai Shiso Taikei 5, Religion and the State, p. 444</ref> Following the failure of the national teaching based on [[Shinto|Shinto]] and [[Confucianism]] by the [[Missionary Office]] established within the Shinto priesthood, the largest religious force of the time, [[Buddhism]], especially [[Jōdo Shinshū]], which was the largest religious force at the time. While implementing modern religious policies such as the lifting of the prohibition of [[Christianity]] and the lifting of [[Nyonin Kinsei]] in shrines and temples, the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve the national indoctrination that was required by the Interlocutors. In order to realize national indoctrination, which the [[Department of Divinities]] was unable to achieve, a system of [[Kyodo Shoku]] was established, and the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was used for national indoctrination and [[Taikyo Proclamation|Proclamation of the Great Doctrine]]<ref>Yoshio Yasumaru, Masato Miyaji, eds. Nihon modern thought compendium 5 Religion and the State, p. 431</ref> campaign.
The Ministry of Religion was a [[voluntary sector]] appointment system. Religious figures—such as [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]]s, [[Kannushi]]s, [[Bhikkhu]]s, {{ill|Rakugoka|ja|落語家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakugoka|qq=}}, Waka poets, [[Haiku|haiku poet]]s—were also appointed as instructors. The highest Rank in the Ministry of Education was {{Transliteration|ja|kyosei}}, and in order to further promote the teaching of the people, the [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]] was established to oversee the entire nation, the {{Nihongo|Chukyoin|中教院}} to oversee each prefectural unit, and the {{Nihongo|Shukyoin|小教院}} were established throughout the country. However, the Great Teaching Institute did not achieve much due to serious conflicts of opinion between the Shintoists, who were then up-and-coming, and the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Jodo Shinshu]], who had maintained their old power.
== Issues surrounding the Ministry of Religion == The Ministry of Religion was created in response to the failure of the missionary policies of the Divine Council and the missionary messengers, but ultimately resulted in its abolition. One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{ill|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Defeat the Shogunate Movement|qq=}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position

One of the reasons for this was the confusion in the religious administration at the time. The government initially intended to use the authority and grounding of traditional religions to defend Christianity and to teach the people about the new system after the Restoration, but in order to establish a modern [[State (polity)|state]], the [[Separation of church and state]] and [[freedom of religion]] as basic policies of the state were inevitable, and the lifting of the ban on Christianity became essential for diplomatic relations with Western countries. In addition, the government was skeptical of the existence of the Ministry of Religion, believing that the objectives of missionary policy could be achieved through the establishment of [[Gakusei|The Modern School System]], and the above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move left and right. The above confusion caused the existing religious forces to move to the right and left. In the midst of such confusion, Buddhist forces, which had been outnumbered by the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], supported the [[Meiji Restoration]], especially the {{ill|Defeat the Shogunate Movement|ja|倒幕運動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Defeat the Shogunate Movement|qq=}} However, the [[Ikkō-shū]] led to a growing opposition to the government from within the Jōdo Shinshu sect, and the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock. When the Ministry's measures ran into a deadlock, the Jodo Shinshu movement withdrew from the organization from the standpoint of separation of church and state.
== The Great Teaching Institute's Temple Issue == {{Main|Great Teaching Institute}} The [[:en:Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was the national governing body for the Ministry of Religion, was established within [[Zōjō-ji|Zojoji Temple]] in [[Shiba, Tokyo]]. The establishment of the Great Teaching Institute was a Buddhist initiative, and Zojoji's donation of facilities was voluntary, but when Shinto forces began to take control, the temple refused to accept the donation. However, the dedication of Zojoji Temple went ahead as planned, and the main hall of Zojoji Temple was taken over as the Great Teaching Institute and used as the worship hall of the Great Teaching Institute's Temple. Furthermore, the temple's rituals required the participation and worship of priests, which angered some of the Buddhist authorities (some priests, on the other hand, enthusiastically expressed their blessing by displaying banners and other symbols). Later, on January 1, 1874, the old main building of Zojoji Temple was burned down by arsonists from the former Satsuma Clan, who were opposed to the establishment of a shrine in the Buddhist temple. The shintai was temporarily moved to [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]], and then to a temple newly established by Shinto forces at the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]].
== Officials ==
Secretary of Education
* Saga Sanae (1872) * [[Ōki Takatō|Oki Takatō]] (1872–1873)
'''Daisuke Kobe'''
* Fukuha Mitsushige (former Shinto Daisuke - 1872) * Shinji (1872–1877)
'''Teaching position'''
* Reference to the teaching position
